content.text: Why Claude Services Need a Release Branch Strategy

For many web applications, continuous deployment from trunk is the ideal: every merge to main deploys to production within minutes. Claude services sometimes cannot follow this model. Enterprise customers may be on quarterly upgrade cycles. Regulated environments require deployment artefact sign-off before each release. A new model version may require a coordinated promotion across prompt, tool schema, and infrastructure changes that cannot safely land one commit at a time.

A release branch is a snapshot of trunk taken at a point in time, stabilised by cherry-picking hotfixes, and tagged with semantic version numbers. It allows the team to support multiple deployed versions simultaneously without keeping all of their differences on trunk.

This sub-subtopic defines when release branches are justified for Claude services, how to cut and name them, how hotfixes flow between trunk and release branches, and how to manage prompt and eval artefacts across release branches.
Release Branch Flow Diagram

attrs.alt: Release branch flow: trunk advances continuously; release branches are cut at a point in time, stabilised with cherry-picked hotfixes, and tagged. Two concurrent release branches shown (2025-Q2-v1 and 2025-Q3-v1).

content.text: Two release branches are shown. The first (release/2025-Q2-v1) was cut from trunk at commit 2, received two hotfix cherry-picks from later trunk commits, and was tagged v1.2.1. The second (release/2025-Q3-v1) was cut from trunk at commit 4, received one hotfix cherry-pick, and was tagged v1.3.0. Both exist simultaneously while trunk continues advancing.
When Release Branches Are Justified
Release branches add operational overhead. Use them only when the deployment model genuinely requires them.
Situation
Release branch justified?
Reason
Enterprise customer on quarterly upgrade cycle
Yes
Customer cannot accept continuous trunk deployments
Regulated environment requiring deployment sign-off
Yes
Sign-off artefact must reference a fixed, immutable version
Model version upgrade coordinated across services
Yes
Multiple services must promote together; trunk cannot be the coordination point
SaaS product with continuous deployment
No
Feature flags + canary rollout handle staged releases without branches
Internal tooling
No
No external versioning requirement; trunk is sufficient
Branch Naming Convention

# Pattern: release/<year>-<cadence>-v<major>
# Examples for quarterly cadence:
git checkout -b release/2025-Q2-v1
git checkout -b release/2025-Q3-v1

# Semantic versioning on tags:
# MAJOR = breaking change to tool schema or API contract
# MINOR = new prompt feature or tool added, backward-compatible
# PATCH = hotfix only

git tag v1.2.0   # initial cut of release/2025-Q2-v1
git tag v1.2.1   # after first hotfix cherry-pick
git tag v1.2.2   # after second hotfix cherry-pick
git tag v1.3.0   # initial cut of release/2025-Q3-v1
Cutting a Release Branch
Cutting is a deliberate action. Three steps: cut from trunk HEAD, run the full eval suite, then create the release manifest and initial version tag.

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/cut-release-branch.sh <branch-name> <version>
BRANCH="${1:?Usage: $0 <branch-name> <version>}"
VERSION="${2:?Usage: $0 <branch-name> <version>}"

git fetch origin main
git checkout main && git pull --ff-only origin main
git checkout -b "$BRANCH"
git push -u origin "$BRANCH"

echo "Branch ${BRANCH} cut from $(git rev-parse HEAD)."
echo "Run: python3 scripts/run_evals.py --full --fail-on-must-not-do"
echo "Then: python3 scripts/create_release_manifest.py --version ${VERSION} --branch ${BRANCH}"
echo "Then: git tag v${VERSION} && git push origin v${VERSION}"

# scripts/create_release_manifest.py
import json, hashlib, subprocess
from pathlib import Path
from datetime import datetime, timezone

def create_release_manifest(version: str, branch: str) -> dict:
    prompt_text = Path("prompts/system_prompt.json").read_text()
    prompt_hash = hashlib.sha256(prompt_text.encode()).hexdigest()[:16]

    spec = json.loads(Path("specs/behaviour_spec.json").read_text())
    spec_version = spec.get("version", "unknown")

    git_sha = subprocess.run(
        ["git", "rev-parse", "HEAD"], capture_output=True, text=True
    ).stdout.strip()

    manifest = {
        "version": version,
        "branch": branch,
        "git_sha": git_sha,
        "cut_at": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
        "prompt_hash": prompt_hash,
        "behaviour_spec_version": spec_version,
        "model": "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    }
    Path("release_manifest.json").write_text(json.dumps(manifest, indent=2))
    print(f"Manifest written: {manifest}")
    return manifest

if __name__ == "__main__":
    import argparse
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--version", required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--branch", required=True)
    args = parser.parse_args()
    create_release_manifest(args.version, args.branch)
Hotfix Flow: Trunk First, Then Cherry-Pick

All fixes must land on trunk first, then be cherry-picked to the release branch. Never write a fix directly on the release branch — if the fix is not on trunk, it will regress the next time a new release branch is cut.
Fix on trunk
: open a fix/ branch from main, fix the issue, run the eval suite, merge to main.
Identify cherry-pick targets
: determine which release branches need the fix. Not all fixes apply to all release branches.
Cherry-pick to the release branch
: pick the specific fix commit from trunk.
Re-run evals on the release branch
: the cherry-pick may interact with release-branch-specific prompt changes. Run the full eval suite on the release branch.
Tag a new patch version
: if the release branch is already deployed, tag after evals pass.

# Trunk-first hotfix flow
git checkout main
git checkout -b fix/escalation-nil-guard
# ... fix ...
git commit -m "fix: guard against nil confidence score in escalation logic"
# merge to main via PR ...

FIX_SHA=$(git log main --format="%H" --grep="nil-guard" -1)

# Cherry-pick to Q2 release branch
git checkout release/2025-Q2-v1
git cherry-pick "$FIX_SHA"
python3 scripts/run_evals.py --full --fail-on-must-not-do
git tag v1.2.2 && git push origin release/2025-Q2-v1 && git push origin v1.2.2

# Cherry-pick to Q3 if applicable
git checkout release/2025-Q3-v1
git cherry-pick "$FIX_SHA"
python3 scripts/run_evals.py --full --fail-on-must-not-do
git tag v1.3.1 && git push origin release/2025-Q3-v1 && git push origin v1.3.1
Prompt and Eval Artefacts on Release Branches
Each release branch carries its own snapshot of the prompt, behaviour spec, and eval suite. The trunk versions of these files may have moved forward since the release branch was cut.
prompts/system_prompt.json
: the exact prompt version for this release. Never overwrite with trunk's version unless explicitly updating the release.
specs/behaviour_spec.json
: the spec version signed off for this release.
release_manifest.json
: records prompt hash, spec version, and git SHA at cut time.
specs/eval_suite.json
: the eval cases that were green at the time of the last tag.

# scripts/verify_release_branch_integrity.py
import json, sys
from pathlib import Path

def verify() -> bool:
    spec = json.loads(Path("specs/behaviour_spec.json").read_text())
    eval_suite = json.loads(Path("specs/eval_suite.json").read_text())

    spec_version = spec.get("version", "unknown")
    eval_spec_ref = eval_suite.get("spec_version", "")

    if eval_spec_ref != spec_version:
        print(
            f"FAIL: eval_suite references spec v{eval_spec_ref} "
            f"but behaviour_spec is v{spec_version}. "
            "Update eval suite spec_version before tagging."
        )
        return False

    print(f"OK: Release branch integrity verified (spec v{spec_version})")
    return True

if __name__ == "__main__":
    sys.exit(0 if verify() else 1)
Testing Across Concurrent Release Branches

When two release branches are live simultaneously, the same hotfix must be validated independently on each branch. A fix that passes evals on Q2 may not pass on Q3 because the surrounding prompt context is different.
CI Configuration for Release Branches
Release branches run a modified CI pipeline: the eval suite always runs in full mode, and the deploy target is the environment corresponding to that release version.

# .github/workflows/release-branch-ci.yml
name: Release Branch CI
on:
  push:
    branches: ["release/**"]

jobs:
  lint-and-type:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: "3.12" }
      - run: pip install ruff mypy anthropic pydantic
      - run: ruff check . && mypy src/ --ignore-missing-imports

  eval-suite-full:
    needs: [lint-and-type]
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    env:
      ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: "3.12" }
      - run: pip install anthropic pydantic
      - name: Full eval suite — release branches never run partial
        run: python3 scripts/run_evals.py --full --fail-on-must-not-do

  release-integrity:
    needs: [eval-suite-full]
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: python3 scripts/verify_release_branch_integrity.py

  deploy-to-release-env:
    needs: [release-integrity]
    if: github.event_name == 'push'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Derive deployment environment from branch name
        run: |
          ENV=$(echo "$GITHUB_REF_NAME" | sed 's|release/||' | tr '/' '-')
          echo "Deploying $GITHUB_REF_NAME to staging-${ENV}"
Operational Runbook: Release Branch Incidents
When a production incident is caused by a Claude service on a release branch, the response differs from a trunk-based service because the fix must be coordinated across the release branch and trunk.
Incident Response Steps
Disable the affected feature via kill switch
: do not wait for a fix. Set FF_DISABLE_AI_TRIAGE=true in the production environment for the affected release. This stops the bleeding immediately.
Confirm the scope
: is the incident on one release branch or multiple? Check whether other release branches (Q3, trunk) are also affected.
Reproduce in evals
: find the input that triggers the bad behaviour. Add it as a new eval case before writing any fix.
Fix on trunk
: write the fix on a fix/ branch from main, verify that the new eval case now passes, merge to main.
Cherry-pick to affected release branch(es)
: cherry-pick the fix, run the full eval suite on each target branch.
Re-enable the feature
: set FF_DISABLE_AI_TRIAGE=false after the cherry-picked version passes evals and is deployed.
Tag the new patch version
: tag v1.2.3 (or the next PATCH version) to mark the hotfix deployment.

# Incident response commands (abbreviated)

# Step 1: disable in production
aws ssm put-parameter --name "/prod/FF_DISABLE_AI_TRIAGE" --value "true" --overwrite

# Step 3: add the failing case to the eval suite
# (edit specs/eval_suite.json manually — add the new case)

# Step 4: fix on trunk
git checkout main
git checkout -b fix/INCIDENT-2025-42-confidence-overflow
# ... fix ...
git commit -m "fix: cap confidence score to [0.0, 1.0] range (INCIDENT-2025-42)"
# merge to main via PR

# Step 5: cherry-pick
FIX_SHA=$(git log main -1 --format="%H")
git checkout release/2025-Q2-v1
git cherry-pick "$FIX_SHA"
python3 scripts/run_evals.py --full --fail-on-must-not-do

# Step 6: re-enable
aws ssm put-parameter --name "/prod/FF_DISABLE_AI_TRIAGE" --value "false" --overwrite

# Step 7: tag
git tag v1.2.3 && git push origin release/2025-Q2-v1 && git push origin v1.2.3
Common Pitfalls
Pitfall
Symptom
Fix
Fix written directly on release branch
Fix missing from trunk; regresses in next release cycle
Always fix on trunk first; cherry-pick to release branches
Cherry-pick not re-evaluated on target branch
Fix passes on Q3 branch but breaks must-not-do on Q2 (different prompt context)
Run full eval suite independently on each target branch
Release branch kept after decommission
CI minutes wasted; stale cherry-pick targets accumulate
Delete branch within one week of decommissioning the deployment
Tags deleted along with branch
Audit trail lost; cannot reproduce the exact version deployed
Only delete the branch pointer; keep all version tags permanently
Trunk eval suite copied to release branch
Release branch eval suite tests trunk prompt, not release prompt
Always run the eval suite that lives on the release branch, not the trunk version
Retiring a Release Branch
Retire when the corresponding deployment is decommissioned. Keep all version tags permanently — they are the audit trail.

# Archive the manifest for audit, then delete the branch pointer

cp release_manifest.json archives/release-2025-Q2-v1-manifest.json
git add archives/ && git commit -m "archive: retire release/2025-Q2-v1"
git push origin main

# Delete remote branch (NOT the tags)
git push origin --delete release/2025-Q2-v1

# Confirm tags still present
git tag --list "v1.2.*"
# v1.2.0  v1.2.1  v1.2.2  v1.2.3  — all preserved
Summary

Release branches for Claude services are justified when the deployment model requires simultaneous support for multiple versions: enterprise quarterly upgrades, regulated sign-off environments, or coordinated multi-service model promotions. They are named release/<year>-<cadence>-v<major>, cut from trunk via a script that produces a release manifest recording the prompt hash, behaviour spec version, model, and git SHA. All fixes flow trunk-first, then cherry-picked independently to each affected release branch, with a full eval suite run on each target branch before tagging a new PATCH version. When a production incident strikes, the kill switch disables the feature immediately while the fix is developed and cherry-picked through the normal trunk-first flow. Branches are retired when the deployment is decommissioned, but version tags are kept permanently as an immutable audit trail.
